speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. Paul, we've covered a lot of your stories during the wartime experience to 1945. Can you now give me a summary of the events after the war, from 1945 onwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can do that. My brother, who was my partner before the war, was in Israel. And my family was in Curacao. My sister and brother-in-law were in Canada. And so I had them all over the world, and I was standing there by myself. My brother sent me some teeth, because he thought that's a good line to sell in Holland, which I appreciated. And my parents and sister, from Curacao sent me shoes and clothing to sell in Holland. And all this went to the reception room of the [NON-ENGLISH]. I can't think of the president there, in [NON-ENGLISH] in the Hague. Not the teeth. I didn't know what I did with it. I-- I-- as far as I remember, I didn't sell them. But the men, who was in charge there, one of them lives in Melbourne. But I gave them all this, because we got these people coming from Auschwitz, Bergen-Belsen, and so on, because we are talking of months and months later. And in the beginning, when I gave them the cigarettes-- there was one particular instance, which I'd like to mention. There was this man who came back by the name of [? Calfus-- ?] not [? Calfus. ?] No, that's wrong. I can't think of his name. Anyway, there was this man who came back. And-- was his name [? Calfus? ?] It wasn't [? Calfus. ?] Anyway, he came back, and he said, I've lost my wife and all the children. I said, you didn't lose your wife and all the children, because some of them are in [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I had a [? Benford ?] 1,500 weight. And I was traveling wherever I could with this not pleasant captain, who, by that time, he was a colonel, or lieutenant colonel. But I still had that [? Benford ?] 15-- And I was traveling a lot. And I met his wife and the children coming back from camp. But there were only three out of five. But anyway, he wasn't by himself. And his name might come with me a few minutes later. And this is what I was there in that [NON-ENGLISH] all the time. I got a letter from my very close friend from Switzerland, Mennol Anholt and Thea, that they were in Switzerland. They went out of the [NON-ENGLISH] exchange, one of the few. And they were alive. And I think-- I don't know when they arrived in Holland, but all this was rather emotional. The business-- I mentioned before that Willi Eiger offered me a room. And I got some quotas from-- from the distribution center where I made a living. I could have bought a house-- the most beautiful house-- with 3,000 or 4,000 cigarettes. I had millions of them, but I just felt I couldn't do that, because there was so much poverty in Holland that this didn't-- I wouldn't do that. Anyway, this is-- it didn't happen. We lived very, very well in a two-bedroom one in Voorburg. From Voorburg, we bought an apartment in the [NON-ENGLISH], where we had our first baby, which was Jim. [NON-ENGLISH] 154. And we had our first baby, which was on the first floor. And we enjoyed that very much. My business was Czechoslovakia. I went every two or three weeks to Czechoslovakia, because we didn't have a factory. And my brother arrived, either by the end of-- I think, in the middle of 1946. I'm not sure about it, but around one year later after the end of the war. But I'm not sure about that. He arrived. And we got an-- offices in the [NON-ENGLISH] in The Hague. And I went to Czechoslovakia. And my friend, Barend Boers-- and I like to mention that-- started. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] state for a waste merchant. He started doing compensation reciprocity transaction with Czechoslovakia and all other countries. And his first reciprocity was that he said, I will not do a deal-- because waste was very much in demand in those countries-- if there is not at least 5% or 10% in it for stockings. The stocking manufacturers in Czechoslovakia were not destroyed. There was a factory called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was still intact. And I went to Prague, and we had to make these reciprocity or compensation transaction, which were very, very difficult, because there were 10 lines from Czechoslovakia. There were 8 lines from Holland. We had to balance it. And it was up and down every two weeks to Prague. I can't say that I didn't enjoy this. The trip itself-- flying-- was never my scene. But Prague-- you know, this excitement suited me very, very well. And I can't-- oh, suited me. I was excited, and I was young. And I-- these deals were very interesting. And it got to the stage-- and I must say, I must give it to my friend, Barend Boers, because I'm going to show that to his wife-- the late Barend Boers, to his wife-- that he was a fantastic friend for me. He was so good, and I'm still missing him. I was with him on the boat to Jamaica. I was with him in the plane to New York. I was with him together in Canada. And I was with him together in Dubocourt, Harwich, wherever we were stationed in England. And I'm missing him very much. He was a wonderful, wonderful man. And-- and-- and I-- and-- and I hope-- and I hope, and I think that his relatives in Amsterdam, or wherever they are, that they will-- will remember him as a wonderful, wonderful human being. Anyway, the first transaction which we did with Czechoslovakia was thanks to Barend Boers. And all the other one were beautiful-- very nice. Our first money we made was with Czechoslovakia. My brother came back, as I said before, the second half of 1946. And the business was already on the go. And it got to the stage where we imported ten thousands of dozens, or thousands of dozens. [INAUDIBLE] ten thousands of dozens of stockings. And we were only allowed to make a margin of 20%. They were still on coupon. They called it coupon in Belgium. I don't know what they call it in Holland. I can't think of that. But anyway, we did OK. We made a living. We didn't get a lot out of it. Before that happened, my parents and my sister and brother-in-law sent me some shoes from Curacao and sent me some clothing, you know, to make money. Because in Curacao, they also had everything. Up to the last day, they had it. When it came, it went straight to the [NON-ENGLISH], which you know by now what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there it was. We had a-- a business already. We were in the [NON-ENGLISH]. We had our reciprocity compensation transaction. And what we were missing is-- what we were missing when my brother came? We were missing something which we are more attractive, which was to have a factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we didn't have a factory, so we moved from there to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I think it were number 31. And from there, we did business with Ireland, because my friend Mennol Anholt and Thea Anholt, got worried about the third world war. And as they were during the war time. And to Reginstadt. First, in Westerbork, then to Reginstadt. And I can't blame them for what they did. And we were even thinking, Melitta and myself, to move over to Ireland. We went to Ireland to have a look at it. And it looked to us rather [NON-ENGLISH]-- dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we are talking about 1940-- 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were thinking about moving to Ireland, because it was away from the-- from-- from-- you know, the Cold War started by the Americans, because they are the big power. And so we were thinking of moving over there. But we were not-- we were looking at these houses. And it was rather quiet in-- in the morning, in the evening, and in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, when we was in the hotel, a-- a-- a woman came in. And she saw me, she dropped everything because she was not supposed to serve us when there was a man around. Anyway, this-- it was very, very, according to us, backwards. It wasn't-- because that's the way we saw it, right? And it's-- can I tell you a joke in between, or not? If you'd like.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. There's Moisha, Heim, and going to a gallery. And Moisha said to Heim, what is this for painting? One eye here and one eye there? He said, don't you know? This is a Picasso? This is abstract. That's the way he sees it. Oh, it's very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: They go to another painting. He said, what is this? One breast here, and one breast where the [NON-ENGLISH] is. What is that? He said, that's a Picasso. He got already upset. It's an abstract. That's the way he sees it. So the fellow thought, if he sees it that way, why doesn't-- you know, if he doesn't see well, why does he paint?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right? I didn't tell it well, but it doesn't really matter. OK. He doesn't see well, why does he paint? OK. That's the way it is. So where are we? We are on island. So I came back-- How many children had you by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this stage, my first son was born in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1st of September. My second child was born. Did you understand that joke? You didn't understand it. My second child was born, Yvonne, in 1948. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we're getting now to the stage where I got worried. Right? I got worried because, in 1946, when I went the first time to Prague, I met a man called Jan Laos, Jan Laos, a Dutchman. He was the representative in Prague of the AKU. And AKU was one of the-- the second-largest organization, after Phillips. I'm looking at you, Nicole, when I-- right? The second-largest organization in-- in Holland. They were making yarn, and he was a representative.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1948, if you remember, the communists came into power. And I had a lot-- I went there every two or three weeks for compensation and-- and reciprocity transaction. We had this fellow called [? Gamka. ?] We had another one. You know, we had these fellows which were organized in that. And thanks to Barend Boers, the first deal I did because of him. And I can thank him. And I would like to thank him again, because his children are going to see this video. Anyway, so to go back to Czechoslovakia-- my friend was a friend of the trade commissioner by the name of [INAUDIBLE] Lachman, who introduced me to him. He got imprisoned in 1949 in Czechoslovakia. And he got imprisoned by doing things which was not supposed to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was that? That was he had-- they found in his-- in his place a-- a-- some-- a-- a-- a map with some red-- Markers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --flags. And they thought this was because he was a spy. Now, my friend was not pro-communist. He was Catholic. Not only that, he was very anti-communist. The same time, we had some Jewish people which couldn't get out of Czechoslovakia. We had an organization-- we had an organization-- we had a transport company in Holland called, Van Gend and Loos. Van Gend and Loos-- and I will spell that. V-A-N, one word. Gend is G-E-N-D. And Loos is L-O-O-S. Still the largest-- one of the largest transport company in Holland. They went up and down with-- it was one of the first with they have a double containers-- [INAUDIBLE] double trucks, and so on, and so on. And I needed some goods to get out there from these Jewish people, and he helped me with it. This started in '47-- no, '48, when the communists came in there. And he was imprisoned in-- in middle or late '49. And we got him out. We got these people-- sorry, we got these people, whenever they want to get out of Czechoslovakia, we got it out with Van Gend and Loos. The man was imprisoned the second half of 1949, accused of being a spy. At the same time, there were two people in Budapest which were accused of being a spy. One was [? Voglers, ?] and the other was [? Sanders. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jan Laos, Jan Laos, was working for AKU, which was second-- second or third large organization in Holland. The [? Vogler ?] was-- I think it was General Electric. And the other one, [? Sanders, ?] was with a similar company, I think. And I'm not sure about it-- was CIS, or something like that-- large company. All three [INAUDIBLE] reminded me of the 1934 Reichstag process of-- I think it was [? Luber. ?] Was it [? Luber? ?] No, you don't know. You didn't read about it. You were too young. You were born just recently. But anyway, this was [? Vonda Luber, ?] right? And it reminded me of that. They took these people as an example to do what they had to do. There was Paul, who saw this Jan Laos every two weeks, or every week. And he was sentenced to death. And I couldn't go to Czechoslovakia anymore, because I was partly-- I thought I was partly responsible in helping him to get these girls out of where they were, right? Girls or men, whoever it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time, at home, in the [NON-ENGLISH] 154-- my brain is still OK-- we had suitcases in our store room, all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't touch your microphone. Your microphone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sorry. Did I touch that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't touch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. Don't touch. OK. All over. And one particular incident I want to tell you. There was this particular one who came to our place. And she told me that she's missing her fur coat. Looked all the-- all the suit cases. I couldn't find her fur coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her, how much what it was? She said, $3,000. I said, I'll pay you $3,000. No. She said they has to discuss it with her sister. They arrived. [INAUDIBLE] They sent the suitcase, and then they came. It might be worth more. Then she came already $5,000, and then $7,000. I said, all right. I-- no, that one day, I went to the synagogue in the city, the Shabbat. And I walked back. And there was this furrier. And he called me. And he said, what's happened with that fur which you deposited here, and I had to keep it clean? That was her fur. So she took it. The value, probably, was only $500, or $1,000. But this is what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, Jan Laos, was sentenced to death in 1949. I think what happened to [? Sanders ?] and [? Vogler, ?] probably the same. I worked towards the Dutch government. They put an embargo toward Czechoslovakia. Czechoslovakia was the port of-- of-- Holland was the port of Czechoslovakia, so there was no goods coming through anymore. What they did with Hungary-- England and America put a embargo towards Hungary. That was also the problem. We went to a fortune teller with Nell Laos, his wife in Holland. And the person put her hands on her and said, she will stay 19 months in prison. And then, he will be liberated. We laughed about it. He said, he has got pain in his leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it wasn't the fortune teller, it was somebody close to it. He's got pain in his legs. And he can't walk. He is in a room of three by five, or something like that. You know something? He came three days before the 19 months. He came out. And I put the newspaper there. And I gave him the reception in Dutch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that was the end of our Czechoslovakian deals. In the meantime, we started Mennol and Thea, which were-- which we were rightly-- they were-- we were all expecting the third world war, because America did not accept the fact that Russia was as strong as they were. Just throw that in, as my opinion. And they left for Ireland. We did some business with Ireland. And our company was called IPKO. I was in very good terms with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why I left Holland-- that's what you really want to know. Surely. I left Holland for two reasons, because my friend came out, Jan Laos, in 1951, maybe beginning of '52. I also saw-- I also was on the-- how would I say? I also was on the reserve of the Army. And I didn't want to go. I was so many years in the Army, I didn't want to go there anymore. And I couldn't understand why Germany, after the war, got as strong as they were, why Japan got as strong as they were. And why do I have to fight again? The whole thing didn't make sense to me. It was a political situation. Now power-- why is this power? There was no power [INAUDIBLE] you know? And-- and-- and I don't want to go to-- into politics, but I think something along the line was wrong. So our decision was to get away from Holland, to go ahead away from Holland. Jan Laos was free, right? He was liberated after 19 months in prison. What year did you leave Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking, he came out by the end-- the second half of 19-- no, he came-- no, he came out the beginning of 1952, or at the end of '51, beginning of 1952. So our decision was, are we going to Canada? Are we going to Central America? Are we going to South America? No, the furthest away is New Zealand. We're going to New Zealand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, Jan-- he was without a job. I mean, they still paid him his salary, AKU, which was a large company. But he went-- I said, you go to New Zealand. This is the furthest away from Europe. Is so far away that, you know, whatever happen in the third world war, maybe we got a chance there. Don't forget, we are talking about the atom bomb, which came down to Hiroshima. So he went to New Zealand. He rang me. I could hardly hear his voice because, at the time, the telephone wasn't working as well as it does now. And we were screaming to each other. And he said, this is the end of the world. This is not what you're going to like. But I came along a place called Sydney, which is very cosmopolitan. That would be for you. Because he was a man from the country. That would be ideal for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, fix up. I had two children. He had three children. Go to Canberra-- that's the one thing I knew-- Canberra was the capital of Australia-- and fix up the papers. And that's exactly what he did. He went from-- he fixed up the papers. He came back, and we went-- we left-- not Holland. We went from Italy. My brother was very upset. Was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very upset, because we never had a fight. You know, once a year, we had a fight. He was pushing the money towards me. I mean, you know, visual-- not visual but, you know. And I was pushing, to him. You know, I never had to fight with my brother. He was a wonderful man, and I'm missing him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we left, and he couldn't understand that. He didn't touch my office-- and I've got photos there to show you in my office-- for 10 years. There was a cleaning woman was allowed to go in there to clean it once every week. He didn't want me to leave. He said, my brother's coming back. The fact that I remained-- that I stayed in Australia was that I had a bit of-- how would I say? I-- I was proud. I'm not going to give in. When did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Australia, again, in November-- the 2nd November, 1952. And if it wouldn't have been for my brother and for Barend Boers, and Mennol, who made bets-- no, Mennol didn't make a bet. They all made bets that I'm coming back within six months. There was no doubt about it, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm coming back. And I said, I'm not leaving this country. I was-- I mean, can you imagine, 1953, going to a milk bar here and so on? It's not a milk bar. We were not used to go to cafes and restaurant with two little children. But it was really difficult. And nobody was waiting for me. And I want to tell you the story how I started my company, called Kolotex. Can I tell you that story? And I think this is what we should do at the moment, because this is, for me, very important to tell you that story. I'd like you to tell me that complete story on our next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
